name: Sleipner CO2 Storage
source: Global CCS Institute Facilities Database 2023; IEEFA Norway Sleipner & Snøhvit CCS report Jun 2023
notes: Operational since 1996; CO2 stripped from Sleipner West natural gas field and injected into Utsira Sand saline aquifer ~800-1000m depth. Over 25 Mt total stored as of 2025. First industrial-scale dedicated CO2 storage project globally. Avg ~1.0 Mtpa injection rate.

name: Snøhvit CO2 Storage
source: Global CCS Institute Facilities Database 2023; IEEFA Norway CCS report Jun 2023; Lessons Learned 14 years CCS Operations (GHGT 2012)
notes: Operational since 2008, Barents Sea LNG. CO2 injected into Tubasen sandstone formation offshore. Injection briefly suspended 2011 due to pressure buildup; resumed in alternate reservoir. Design ~0.7 Mtpa.

name: Northern Lights CCS (Phase 1)
source: TotalEnergies press releases 2024-2025; Equinor Northern Lights project page; ClearBlue Markets Aug 2025; norlights.com
notes: JV Equinor/Shell/TotalEnergies. Phase 1 capacity 1.5 Mtpa; offshore terminal completed 2024; first CO2 injection from Heidelberg Materials cement plant began summer 2025. Phase 1 fully booked. CO2 stored 2600m below seabed in saline aquifer. Phase 2 expansion targeting 5+ Mtpa planned.

name: Quest CCS (Shell Scotford, Alberta)
source: Global CCS Institute Facilities Database 2023; CCS Knowledge Centre Alberta review 2024
notes: Operational since 2015. Captures CO2 from Shell Scotford hydrogen unit (Athabasca oil sands upgrader). Stores in Basal Cambrian Sands saline aquifer, Alberta. ~1 Mtpa consistently; cited as first commercial CCS on oil sands.

name: Weyburn-Midale CO2-EOR & Storage
source: Global CCS Institute Facilities Database 2023; PNAS 2013 doi:10.1073/pnas.1302156110; MIT Carbon Sequestration Project Database
notes: Williston Basin, Saskatchewan. CO2-EOR since 2000; CO2 sourced via pipeline from North Dakota Great Plains Synfuels plant. ~3 Mtpa injection rate; ~20 Mt expected over project life. Extensive monitoring demonstrates permanent storage. EOR-primary but CO2 permanently stored.

name: Illinois Industrial CCS (ADM Decatur)
source: Global CCS Institute Facilities Database 2023; MIT Sequestration Project Database; DOE NETL Illinois ICCS project page
notes: ADM ethanol plant, Decatur, Illinois. Demo phase 2011-2014 stored 1 Mt; commercial Illinois ICCS phase operational at ~1 Mtpa into Mt. Simon Sandstone saline aquifer (Illinois Basin). No CO2 leakage detected in monitoring. Flagship US dedicated storage project.

name: Gorgon CCS (Chevron, Barrow Island)
source: IEEFA Gorgon CCS Underperformance Nov 2024; Chevron Australia CCS fact sheet; Wikipedia Gorgon CO2 Injection Project; Rigzone Nov 2024
notes: Nominal largest CCS project globally by design capacity (4 Mtpa). Operational since Aug 2019 but significantly underperforming: actual injection ~1.3-1.6 Mtpa in FY2024 due to reservoir pressure management issues (water production constraints). >10 Mt total injected as of mid-2024; only ~44% of required CO2 captured FY2019-2024. Chevron Barrow Island, Western Australia. CO2 stripped from Gorgon LNG; stored in Dupuy Formation saline aquifer.

name: Santos Moomba CCS
source: Santos press releases Oct 2024, May 2026; Enerdata news Oct 2024; Rigzone May 2026
notes: Commenced operations October 2024. Australia's first large-scale CCS hub. CO2 from Moomba gas processing plant injected into depleted gas reservoirs, Cooper Basin (South Australia). ~340,000 t stored by Dec 2024; ~1 Mt in first full year. Record Australian Carbon Credit Units awarded 2025.

name: In Salah CCS (Algeria)
source: Global CCS Institute; MIT Sequestration Fact Sheet; In Salah Lessons Learned ScienceDirect 2013 doi:10.1016/j.egypro.2013.06.002; CSLF project record
notes: Operated BP/Sonatrach/Equinor 2004-2011. CO2 stripped from Krechba natural gas field injected into Carboniferous sandstone ~1.9 km depth, Sahara onshore. ~3.8 Mt stored before injection suspended 2011 due to InSAR-detected caprock micro-deformation / seal integrity concerns. Now in long-term monitoring. Classed as operational for project lifetime but active injection ended.

name: Sinopec Qilu-Shengli CCUS
source: Sinopec press release Jan 2022; US DOE Workshop presentation Jun 2023; SASAC China statement Sep 2022
notes: China's first million-tonne CCUS project. CO2 captured at Qilu Petrochemical (Shandong), transported via pipeline and injected into Shengli Oilfield (Bohai Bay Basin) for EOR. Operational January 2022. 10.68 Mt total projected over 15 years; ~3 Mt incremental oil production expected. Storage type is EOR in sandstone reservoir.

name: Tomakomai CCS Demonstration (Japan)
source: JCCS Tomakomai CCS Demonstration brochure 2018; DOE June 2023 workshop; Argus Media 2024; CSLF project record
notes: Japan's first offshore CCS demo. Operated by Japan CCS Co. (JCCS). Injected into Moebetsu saline formation; 300,012 t cumulative by Nov 2019 when demo injection ended. Capacity ~0.1 Mtpa for demonstration phase. Commercial-scale development (1.5-2 Mtpa) planned by Idemitsu/Japex/Hokkaido Electric. Japan CCS regulatory framework enacted May 2024.

name: Al Reyadah CCS (UAE)
source: Global CCS Institute Facilities Database 2023; Springer Arabian J Sci Eng MENA CCUS review 2025
notes: ADNOC/Masdar JV. Captures CO2 from Emirates Steel direct-reduced-iron facility in Abu Dhabi. Injected into Abu Dhabi onshore oil fields for EOR. ~800,000 t/yr. First large-scale CCS in Middle East and MENA. Expansion plans to 10 Mtpa by 2030 announced.

name: CarbFix / Hellisheidi Basalt Storage (Iceland)
source: UNESCO operational report; Carbon Credits article 2024; PMC Rapid CO2 mineralization study 2019; CarbFix first EU onshore CCS permit
notes: Hellisheidi geothermal power plant, ON Power/CarbFix. CO2 dissolved in water and injected into Icelandic basalt where it mineralizes to stable carbonate minerals within ~2 years. Initial pilot ~2,200 t/yr; current ~12,000-50,000 t/yr CO2+H2S (incl. Climeworks DAC offtake). First EU permit for onshore CCS granted. Proof-of-concept for basalt sequestration globally. Basin-scale Iceland basalt capacity estimated at several Gt (see basin entry).

name: Porthos CCS (Netherlands)
source: Porthos project website porthosco2.nl; Offshore Energy news Jan 2025; IOGP Europe CO2 Storage Map Mar 2024
notes: JV EBN/Gasunie/Port of Rotterdam. CO2 from Rotterdam industrial cluster (Shell, ExxonMobil, Air Liquide, Air Products) transported offshore ~20 km into depleted P18 North Sea gas fields. 2.5 Mtpa for 15 years (~37 Mt total). 4 injection wells converted by Jan 2025. First injection expected 2026.

name: Aramis CCS (Netherlands / North Sea)
source: Aramis CCS website aramis-ccs.com; OGCI CCUS Hub profile; Carbon Herald 2024
notes: JV TotalEnergies/Shell/EBN/Gasunie. CO2 transport trunk infrastructure designed for 22 Mtpa transport capacity; Phase 1 storage target ~5 Mtpa in depleted Dutch North Sea gas fields. Intended as backbone Dutch and broader European CCS hub. FID pending as of late 2025.

name: Greensand CCS (Denmark)
source: CATF CCS in Europe Nov 2025; IOGP Europe CO2 Storage Map May 2025
notes: First full-value-chain CCS in continental Europe. Demo phase March 2023 (Greensand Demo). Commercial 'Greensand Future' phase FID December 2024; targeting ~0.4 Mtpa from early 2026. Nini oil field, Danish North Sea. Expansion planned to 8 Mtpa by 2030.

name: Acorn CCS (Scotland / North Sea)
source: UK Government Track-2 CCS funding Jun 2025; Energy Voice Jun 2025; IOGP Europe CO2 Storage Map May 2025; Blackridge Research Acorn profile 2026
notes: Storegga-led. Based at St Fergus gas terminal, NE Scotland. UK Government £200M development funding approved Jun 2025. Full-scale target 10 Mtpa by 2030 across cluster; transport capacity designed for up to 20 Mtpa. Storage in Scottish North Sea saline aquifers and depleted fields.

name: Bayou Bend CCS (SE Texas, Gulf Coast)
source: Chevron Bayou Bend press release Q3 2025; OGCI CCUS Hub profile; Carbon Herald 2025
notes: JV Chevron/TotalEnergies/Equinor. ~140,000 acres pore space in SE Texas saline aquifers. Estimated gross storage potential >1 Gt. Class VI UIC injection permit filed 2024 (~2 yr approval timeline). Intended as flagship Gulf Coast industrial-emitter hub. Capacity figure is indicative annual rate for large-scale hub; total project capacity >1 Gt over decades.

name: Summit Carbon Solutions (US Midwest Ethanol)
source: Global CCS Institute Global Status of CCS 2024; Summit Carbon Solutions company filings
notes: Largest planned CO2 pipeline network in North America. Captures from ~50 ethanol plants across Iowa, Minnesota, Nebraska, North Dakota, South Dakota. Pipeline ~5,000 km to ND saline aquifer storage. ~12 Mtpa design. Landowner and state regulatory permits ongoing 2024-2025. Important BiCRS context: co-located with major corn-belt ag biomass sources.

name: Alberta Carbon Trunk Line (ACTL)
source: Global CCS Institute Facilities Database 2023; Incorrys Alberta CCS review 2024
notes: Operational since 2020. Collects CO2 from Agrium/Nutrien fertilizer and Sturgeon Refinery; transports south to Clive/Joffre oil fields for EOR. Pipeline design capacity 14.6 Mtpa; current throughput ~1.8 Mtpa. CO2 permanently sequestered in Nisku Formation via EOR.

name: Wabash Valley Resources CCS (Illinois Basin)
source: Global CCS Institute Facilities Database 2023; DOE CarbonSAFE program; DOE NETL project records
notes: Fertilizer production plant (ammonia/CO2), Terre Haute area. CO2 to Mt. Simon Sandstone saline aquifer, Illinois Basin. Class VI UIC permit application in progress. Anchor project for IL Basin commercial storage scale-up.

name: US Gulf Coast Saline Aquifers
source: US DOE NETL Carbon Storage Atlas 5th Edition 2015; DOE Energy.gov 2,400 Gt US total estimate; NATCARB Viewer; Gulf Coast Carbon Center UT Austin CarbonSAFE
notes: Miocene-age sandstone formations (Frio, Tuscaloosa, Wilcox) onshore and offshore Texas/Louisiana coast. Among the most favorable storage settings globally: high permeability, thick sequences, proximity to large industrial CO2 sources. P50 estimate ~500 Gt for Gulf Coast alone within US total of ~2,400 Gt. Key BECCS pathway region given dense biomass + co-located industrial emitters.

name: US Midcontinent / Illinois Basin Saline
source: US DOE NETL Carbon Storage Atlas 5th Edition 2015; TGS Illinois Basin assessment 2024; DOE CarbonSAFE Illinois East sub-basin
notes: Mt. Simon Sandstone saline aquifer — thick, continuous, well-characterized. Largest dedicated storage target in US Midwest. Several active injection projects (ADM Decatur, Wabash Valley). Multiple Class VI permit applications filed. High confidence from extensive characterization. Important for corn-belt BECCS/bio-energy-with-CCS given colocation with ethanol plants.

name: Williston Basin (US / Canada)
source: NETL Cambro-Ordovician Saline System (COSS) binational assessment; EDX storage capacity dataset; NETL Atlas 5th Ed
notes: Cambro-Ordovician Saline System (COSS) extends across Williston Basin (ND/SD) into Alberta. P50 effective static CO2 storage = 412 Gt; P10 = 218 Gt, P90 = 706 Gt. Also includes depleted O&G reservoirs (Weyburn-Midale EOR). One of largest storage resources in North America. Summit Carbon Solutions pipeline targets this basin.

name: Permian Basin (Texas / New Mexico)
source: US DOE NETL Carbon Storage Atlas 5th Edition 2015; NATCARB Viewer
notes: Large depleted O&G reservoir network plus deeper saline aquifers. Active CO2-EOR industry gives existing CO2 pipeline infrastructure. Saline aquifer capacity less well-characterized than Illinois or Gulf Coast. Estimate 60 Gt is conservative midpoint for saline + depleted O&G combined. Proximity to Permian unconventional ag region (cotton, sorghum) for potential BECCS.

name: Alberta Basin (Canada)
source: NETL COSS binational Canada-US assessment; Canada Atlas of Geological CO2 Storage (NRCan); Incorrys Alberta CCS review 2024
notes: Shares Cambro-Ordovician Saline System with Williston Basin. Multiple active CCS projects (Quest, ACTL, Weyburn). Alberta Carbon Storage Hub (ATCO/Wolf Midstream) proposed. Strong regulatory framework (Alberta Carbon Trunk Line infrastructure). High confidence from decades of O&G data. Key Canada BECCS corridor.

name: North Sea (UK / Norwegian / Dutch-Danish sectors)
source: EU GeoCapacity project 2009 (114 Gt onshore + 244 Gt offshore Europe total); CO2StoP project 2013 (482 Gt EU saline total); CATF EU CO2 Storage Summary GEUS Report 2021-34; SINTEF Nordic CO2 storage potential
notes: North Sea basin has the largest well-characterized offshore storage in the world. Includes Utsira Sand (Sleipner), Johansen/Smeaheia (Northern Lights), Bunter Sandstone (UK), P18 (Porthos). Estimate 160 Gt for North Sea specifically (UK + Norwegian + Dutch-Danish sectors combined), conservatively derived from CO2StoP EU total with North Sea allocation. Some sources suggest >100 Gt for North Sea alone. Extensive depleted gas fields add further capacity.

name: North Sea (depleted O&G fields, UK+NOR+NLD)
source: CO2StoP project 2013 (25 Gt total EU hydrocarbon fields); CATF EU geological storage summary 2021
notes: 513 hydrocarbon fields assessed in CO2StoP; total EU depleted O&G capacity = 25 Gt, with North Sea fields representing the bulk. Well-characterized. Porthos, Greensand, and Acorn all use this capacity. Smaller than saline aquifer potential but more immediately accessible due to existing data.

name: Baltic Sea Basin (Europe)
source: SINTEF Nordic CO2 storage potential report; EU GeoCapacity project 2009; CO2StoP 2013
notes: Baltic Sea saline aquifer system; multi-Gt range identified in recent evaluations (SINTEF). Potential storage for Poland, Baltic states, Sweden, and Finland. Less developed characterization than North Sea. Moderate confidence. Relevant for Eastern European biomass (Polish forests, Baltic ag residues).

name: Ordos Basin (China)
source: China CO2 storage dataset (Scientific Data / PMC 2025); Tandfonline China CCUS pathways 2022; ScienceDirect China basin assessments
notes: Large sedimentary basin in N-central China (Inner Mongolia/Shaanxi/Ningxia). Major coal and gas production region; multiple active CCS projects targeting saline aquifers. Estimate ~200 Gt saline aquifer capacity (broad range in literature 100-400+ Gt). Important for NW China BECCS co-location with existing coal+biomass co-firing.

name: Songliao Basin (China)
source: China CO2 geological storage dataset (Scientific Data / PMC 2025); ScienceDirect China saline formations assessment
notes: Northeast China (Heilongjiang/Jilin). Largest individual basin capacity in China per recent fine-grid assessment (694 Gt saline). Daqing oilfield also provides EOR potential. Biomass context: major corn production region (corn stover) — strong colocation case for BECCS.

name: Bohai Bay Basin (China)
source: China CO2 geological storage dataset (Scientific Data / PMC 2025); Sinopec Qilu-Shengli CCUS operational data
notes: Eastern China, including Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei region. Onshore + offshore saline aquifer capacity ~490 Gt; offshore sub-basin ~40 Gt separately assessed. Shengli Oilfield EOR (Sinopec Qilu project) operational. Dense industrial CO2 sources nearby. Moderate confidence on large saline figures.

name: Junggar Basin (China)
source: ScienceDirect Junggar Basin suitability assessment 2019; China CCUS pathways Tandfonline 2022
notes: NW China (Xinjiang). Saline aquifer storage assessed as 'high potential' with favorable geology (depth, permeability). Estimate ~100 Gt is midpoint from limited published work. Low confidence due to sparse characterization data. Remote location reduces near-term accessibility.

name: Tarim Basin (China)
source: China CO2 geological storage dataset (Scientific Data / PMC 2025)
notes: Xinjiang, NW China. Very large theoretical saline aquifer capacity (~552 Gt) from fine-grid assessment, but extremely remote, limited infrastructure, and sparse characterization. Low confidence on accessibility. Low near-term BECCS relevance due to sparse biomass in region.

name: Gippsland Basin (Australia, offshore)
source: Geoscience Australia Gippsland Basin CO2 Storage Project; CO2CRC Australian storage atlas; OGCI CO2 Storage Management System (~31 Gt sub-commercial + 470 Gt undiscovered national)
notes: Offshore Victoria, SE Australia. Well-characterized from O&G exploration. Multiple potential storage formations (Latrobe Group). Advanced characterization stage. Proximate to Victoria/NSW biomass (forestry, agricultural residues).

name: Cooper-Eromanga Basin (Australia)
source: Santos Moomba CCS operational data; Geoscience Australia CCS geological storage studies; CO2CRC screening study
notes: South Australia outback. Moomba CCS (operational Oct 2024, 1.7 Mtpa) uses depleted gas reservoirs here. Well-characterized due to decades of gas production. High confidence for commercially accessible capacity near Moomba plant (~8 Gt estimate for saline + depleted combined in Cooper-Eromanga).

name: Carnarvon Basin (Australia, offshore)
source: Geoscience Australia Advanced CO2 Geological Storage Sites 2030 (2021); OGCI CO2 Storage Resource Catalogue; Gorgon CCS operational context
notes: Northern Carnarvon Basin (Barrow Sub-basin) offshore NW Western Australia. Gorgon CCS injects here. Advanced characterization underway per Geoscience Australia 2021 report. Large Miocene and Cretaceous formations. Major storage region for WA's LNG industry CO2.

name: Surat Basin (Queensland, Australia)
source: Geoscience Australia Advanced CO2 Geological Storage Sites 2030 (2021); CO2CRC screening study
notes: SE Queensland. Hutton Sandstone saline aquifer primary target. Advanced characterization stage per Geoscience Australia 2021. Proximate to Queensland coal-belt and agricultural biomass (sugarcane bagasse, cotton gin trash). Important for BECCS in Queensland.

name: Santos / Pre-Salt Basin (Brazil, offshore)
source: ScienceDirect CO2 Geological Storage in Santos Basin 2022; ScienceDirect Pre-Salt CO2-injection review 2024
notes: Offshore SE Brazil. Santos Basin has zones with capacity >6.1 Gt CO2. Pre-salt reservoirs (Campos area) assessed at >3.3 Gt static capacity in four fields under active injection. Brazil's Petrobras already co-injects CO2 from pre-salt gas processing. Potential for BECCS coupling with sugarcane ethanol industry.

name: Campos Basin (Brazil, offshore)
source: ResearchGate CO2 Storage Capacity Campos Basin (2013) via repositorio.pucrs.br; ScienceDirect Pre-Salt CO2 injection review 2024
notes: Offshore Rio de Janeiro state. Depleted oil fields + pre-salt formations. ~3.3 Gt in actively injected pre-salt fields. Petrobras experience with CO2 handling in pre-salt context. Strong candidate for BECCS integration with Brazilian sugarcane/eucalyptus biomass supply.

name: Paraná Basin (Brazil)
source: ResearchGate Parana Basin CO2 storage potential 2022; ResearchGate Perspectives Parana and Santos Basins negative emissions 2022
notes: Onshore SE/S Brazil. Paraná Basin has geological potential near major CO2 point sources (Sao Paulo industrial corridor). Also overlies basalt formations (Paraná flood basalts) with mineral carbonation potential. Storage capacity estimates limited and uncertain — low confidence. Key BiCRS context: SE Brazil has highest sugarcane density globally; storage access is a major pathway-selection factor.

name: Eastern Arabian Basin (Saudi Arabia)
source: ScienceDirect Geological CO2 Storage in Saudi Arabian sedimentary basins 2023; Springer Arabian J Geosciences CCS KSA overview
notes: Eastern Arabian Basin and Interior Homocline-Central Arch most suitable in Saudi Arabia. Deep saline aquifer capacity ~432 Gt at P50; depleted oil reservoirs ~5 Gt + gas ~9 Gt additionally. Large capacity near Riyadh and Eastern Province industrial CO2 sources. World's largest hydrocarbon reservoir system provides additional storage context. Moderate confidence (data dominated by O&G exploration, less saline aquifer characterization).

name: Krishna-Godavari Basin (India)
source: ScienceDirect Systematic CO2 storage capacity assessment India 2021; cemnet.com India CO2 storage tapping potential
notes: East coast India. Largest Category 1 storage site in India. Mid-range capacity 5.78 Gt CO2. Deep saline aquifers in KG basin well-studied for O&G (Krishna-Godavari gas fields). Proximate to Andhra Pradesh / Telangana agricultural residues and coastal biomass. India's most accessible storage formation.

name: Cambay Basin (India)
source: ScienceDirect Systematic CO2 storage capacity assessment India 2021; ScienceDirect Cambay Basin storage complex 2025
notes: Western India (Gujarat). Large theoretical saline aquifer capacity ~74.8 Gt (onshore), but limited characterization for injectivity. Active CCS feasibility study (Gandhar Field saline aquifer). Low confidence due to data gaps and uncertain reservoir quality at scale. Proximate to Gujarat ag-residues and coastal MSW sources.

name: Deccan Basalts (India)
source: ScienceDirect Systematic CO2 storage India 2021; Princeton CMI India Deccan Traps CCS assessment 2022
notes: Covers ~500,000 km2 of central/western India. Theoretical CO2 mineral carbonation capacity potentially very large (97-316 Gt range cited). However Princeton CMI 2022 study indicates practical storage capacity severely limited by low permeability of unaltered basalt — unlikely to be viable storage without significant well-stimulation. CarbFix-style mineralization in Iceland works due to permeable pillow basalts; Deccan Traps are continental flood basalts with lower permeability. Low confidence, low near-term viability.

name: Japan Offshore Saline Aquifers
source: ScienceDirect Saline-aquifer CO2 sequestration in Japan 2011; DOE Workshop Jun 2023 (Tomakomai context)
notes: National assessment by Japan's METI estimated 146 Gt total saline aquifer capacity, but most locations are offshore and distant from onshore CO2 sources. Geographic mismatch is a major practical challenge. Some nearshore formations (e.g. Tomakomai) better located. Low confidence that large theoretical capacity translates to accessible storage at scale near biomass sources. Commercial CCS regulatory framework enacted 2024; exploratory drilling planned from 2025.

name: Southeast Asia Saline Aquifers (ASEAN)
source: IEA CCUS Opportunity in SE Asia report; ADB Prospects for CCS in SE Asia; OGCI CO2 Storage Resource Catalogue Appendix B Asia 2022; APEC CO2 Storage Assessment SE Asia 2010
notes: ASEAN basin-scale theoretical saline capacity estimates range widely (275-629 Gt in some assessments; 100 Gt is a conservative middle estimate for accessible sub-basins). Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, Vietnam have the best-characterized basins (South Sumatra, West Java, Gulf of Thailand, Malay). However, key biomass-rich countries (Indonesia, Philippines, Vietnam, Thailand) have highly uneven storage distribution — major agricultural and forestry biomass regions in Java, Sumatra, Mekong Delta are often distant from viable offshore storage. BiCRS implication: SE Asian biomass (palm, rice, sugarcane, tropical forestry) may be better served by bio-oil/biochar burial pathways than BECCS given storage access constraints.

name: Sub-Saharan Africa — Major Cratonic Regions (Poor Storage)
source: OGCI CO2 Storage Resource Catalogue; limited peer-reviewed basin assessments; CSLF storage gap analysis
notes: CRITICAL STORAGE GAP. Sub-Saharan Africa (outside South Africa and offshore Nigeria/Angola) has very limited characterized CO2 storage. Large cratons (Congo Craton, Tanzania Craton, West African Craton) lack the sedimentary basin thickness needed for deep saline aquifer storage. Nigeria/Gulf of Guinea offshore has some potential but onshore storage near biomass sources is extremely limited. Major BiCRS implication: this region has large biomass potential (tropical forests, savanna crop residues) but near-zero viable geologic CO2 storage => storage-free pathways preferred (biomass burial / Kodama/Graphyte model, biochar, bio-oil with geological disposal elsewhere). Capacity_gt=5 is a generous estimate for accessible onshore formations across the region.

name: Congo Basin (Central Africa — Very Poor Storage)
source: CSLF global storage gap analysis; limited published assessment; Geoscience Australia global storage mapping
notes: CRITICAL STORAGE GAP. Congo Basin is the world's second-largest tropical forest — major biomass carbon stock — but sits on ancient Precambrian craton with shallow sedimentary cover unsuitable for CO2 storage. No viable saline aquifer storage identified. BiCRS implication: Congo basin biomass if mobilized must use storage-free pathways (biomass burial, biochar) or long-distance CO2 transport (impractical). Strongly favors in-situ burial/biochar pathways for any BiCRS in Central Africa.

name: East Africa Rift System
source: Limited academic assessment; analogy with CarbFix Iceland basalt storage; no systematic basin-level study published
notes: East African Rift has extensive basaltic volcanic terrain (Ethiopia, Kenya) that theoretically could host CarbFix-style mineral CO2 storage. But no systematic characterization, no active projects, significant uncertainty. Capacity figure is speculative. Some geothermal activity in Ethiopia/Kenya could support small-scale CarbFix analogues. Not viable at scale near-term. BiCRS implication: E. Africa (major sorghum, coffee, crop residues) likely needs storage-free pathways.

name: West Siberia Basin (Russia)
source: IPCC SRCCS Chapter 5 (2005); global saline aquifer estimates; no systematic published Russian national assessment
notes: West Siberian Basin is one of the world's largest sedimentary basins with massive theoretical storage capacity (~300 Gt estimate). However, extremely remote, permafrost complications, no active CCS projects, and no accessible systematic characterization data. Low confidence on capacity and near-zero near-term accessibility. Russia has not prioritized CCS. BiCRS implication: Russian biomass (massive boreal forest + steppe agriculture) currently cannot access viable storage — favors in-situ burial or biomass preservation pathways.

name: Iceland Basalt (National)
source: Academia.edu CO2 storage potential of basaltic rocks in Iceland and oceanic ridges; CarbFix operational data; calc: Iceland basalt coverage ~130,000 km2 × estimated mineralization capacity
notes: Iceland is almost entirely basaltic with highly permeable pillow lavas. CarbFix demonstrates rapid (<2 yr) permanent mineralization. Large national storage capacity relative to Iceland's emissions. Also relevant to broader basalt storage potential along Mid-Atlantic Ridge. Biomass context: minimal domestic biomass, but important proof-of-concept for DAC+basalt (Climeworks/CarbFix Mammoth).

name: North Sea Norwegian Sector Saline (Deep)
source: SINTEF CO2 Storage Potential in the Nordic Region; Equinor/GEUS Smeaheia/Johansen characterization; Northern Lights geology reports
notes: Norwegian North Sea and mid-Norway offshore — Johansen, Smeaheia, Utsira formations. Well-characterized from O&G exploration and CCS projects. Northern Lights uses Johansen Formation. SINTEF estimated 'large amounts' in Nordic offshore; 80 Gt is conservative allocation to Norwegian sector. Offshore CO2 storage potentially available to all European emitters via shipping (Northern Lights model).

name: Ravenna CCS Phase 1 (Eni/Snam, Adriatic)
source: Eni/Snam press release Sep 2024; Offshore Magazine Oct 2024 (offshore CO2 injection underway); CCSA case study 2024; OnePetro OMC-2025-675 (first 8 months of operations)
notes: Italy's first CCS project; Phase 1 operational since Aug 2024. CO2 captured from Eni gas treatment plant at Casalborsetti (Ravenna municipality), transported offshore ~20 km and injected into Porto Corsini Mare Ovest depleted gas reservoir at ~3,000 m depth, Adriatic Sea. Phase 1 capacity 0.025 Mtpa (25,000 tpa); capture efficiency >90%. Phase 2 targets ~4 Mtpa by 2030, potential expansion to 16 Mtpa as Mediterranean decarbonisation hub. Coordinates reflect offshore injection point (offshore from lat 44.51°N, 12.23°E Casalborsetti).

name: Northern Endurance Partnership / Endurance (East Coast Cluster, UK)
source: NEP press release Dec 2024 (FID / financial close); Offshore Magazine Dec 2024; UK government CCS announcement Jun 2025 (£9.4B capital budget); East Coast Cluster website
notes: UK's first CCS project to achieve financial close (Dec 2024). CO2 collected from Teesside and Humber industrial emitters (East Coast Cluster) and stored in Endurance saline aquifer ~75 km east of Flamborough Head, ~1,000 m below North Sea seabed. Phase 1 capacity 4 Mtpa; long-term ambition up to 27 Mtpa by mid-2030s. Transport and storage infrastructure developed by bp, Equinor, TotalEnergies (NEP). Construction from mid-2025; start-up 2028. Coordinates approximate for Endurance storage site.

name: Liverpool Bay CCS / HyNet T&S (Eni, Irish Sea)
source: Eni FID press release Apr 2025; SPE JPT Apr 2025; Liverpool Bay T&S website liverpoolbay-ts.co.uk; UK government Jun 2025 (£21.7B over 25 years for Track-1 clusters)
notes: FID reached Apr 2025. Eni operator. Stores CO2 in depleted Hamilton, Hamilton North, and Lennox offshore gas fields (Irish Sea, west of Wirral Peninsula). Phase 1 capacity 4.5 Mtpa; expandable to 10 Mtpa by 2030s. Serves HyNet North West industrial cluster. Construction 2025; start-up 2028. Coordinates approximate for Hamilton field area.

name: Viking CCS (Harbour Energy / bp, Southern North Sea)
source: Harbour Energy Viking CCS website; Energy Voice 2024; New Civil Engineer Apr 2025 (DCO granted); Global CCS Institute Status Report 2024; Viking CCS independently verified 300 Mt storage capacity
notes: UK Track-2 project. Development consent order (DCO) granted Apr 2025. Depleted Viking gas fields ~140 km offshore in Southern North Sea, 2.7 km below seabed. Independently verified 300 Mt CO2 storage capacity. Target 10 Mtpa by 2030. Partners: Harbour Energy (operator), bp, RWE, Phillips 66, VPI. Start-up target 2027+ pending FID. Ship-based CO2 transport agreement signed with Cory/ABP.

name: Bifrost CCS Phase 1 (TotalEnergies/DUC, Harald West & Dagny, Denmark)
source: Bifrost CCS website bifrost-ccs.com; TotalEnergies Bifrost press releases 2024-2025; EUDP funding announcement; Enerdata Dec 2024; EU PCI designation
notes: EU Project of Common Interest. Distinct from existing 'Greensand CCS (Denmark)' site (which uses Nini West). Bifrost Phase 1 uses Harald West depleted gas field and Dagny saline structure ~250 km offshore west Denmark. Phase 1 target ≥5 Mtpa from ~2030; total potential 10-15 Mtpa; estimated 335 Mt total storage. Consortium: TotalEnergies (coordinator), BlueNord, Nordsøfonden, Ørsted, DTU. Coordinates approximate for Harald West / Dagny license area.

name: Petrobras Santos Basin Pre-Salt CO2 Reinjection (Brazil)
source: Offshore Energy 2025 (22 FPSOs reinjection record); Rio Times Online 2024 (28% global CCS); ScienceDirect Springer J Pet Explor Prod Tech 2021 (doi 10.1007/s13202-021-01102-8); MIT CCS project: Lula
notes: World's largest CO2 injection program by annual volume. 22 FPSOs in Santos Basin pre-salt (SE Brazil) equipped with CO2 separation and reinjection systems (produced gas contains 8-15% CO2). Record 14.2 Mt CO2 reinjected in 2024 alone (est. ~28% of world CCS capacity that year). Static storage capacity estimated >3.3 Gt in four primary fields (Lula, Sapinhoa, Buzios, Tupi). Primarily EOR/production-management rather than dedicated CCS, but substantial permanent storage occurs. Centroid coordinate approximate for Santos Basin pre-salt polygon offshore Rio de Janeiro state.

name: CNPC Jilin CO2-EOR CCS (Songyuan, China)
source: Global CCS Institute 2018 press release (world's 18th large-scale facility); CNPC 2018 CCS-EOR presentation; MIT CCS project: Jilin; CO2 EOR Jilin ScienceDirect 2014 (doi 10.1016/j.petrol.2014.12.009)
notes: China's first commercial CO2-EOR CCS project. CO2 sourced from Changling natural gas processing plant (~22.5% CO2 in gas), transported by pipeline to Jilin oilfield for EOR. Operational as pilot 2008; commercial scale (0.6 Mtpa) from 2018. Cumulative injection >1.1 Mt. Jilin oilfield located in Songyuan district, Jilin Province, NE China. Coordinates approximate for Jilin oilfield area.

name: CNOOC Enping 15-1 Offshore CCS (Pearl River Mouth Basin, China)
source: CNOOC commissioning Jun 2023; Offshore Magazine 2023; CGTN Sep 2025 (>100M m3 stored); The Chemical Engineer 2023; South China Morning Post 2023
notes: China's first offshore CCS project. Located in Pearl River Mouth Basin, South China Sea, ~190 km SW of Hong Kong, water depth ~80 m. CO2 separated from Enping 15-1 oilfield production gas and injected into saline layer ~800 m below seabed. Peak capacity ~0.3 Mtpa; cumulative storage >100 million m3 as of Sep 2025. Largest offshore oil production platform in Asia. Coordinates approximate for Enping 15-1 block (GEM Wiki: lat ~21.5, lon ~115.1).

name: Guohua Jinjie CCS (China Energy/CHN, Shaanxi)
source: Global CCS Institute Jan 2021; China Energy Investment Corp announcement Jan 2021; ScienceDirect (doi 10.1016/j.seppur.2023.124867); Global Energy Observatory (lat 38.7363, lon 110.1668)
notes: China's largest post-combustion CCS demonstration project at coal-fired power plant. Operational since Jan 2021. Located at Guohua Jinjie power station, Shenmu, Yulin, Shaanxi. Captures 150,000 tpa CO2 from coal power flue gas at >90% capture rate, injected into nearby oilfield for EOR. Coordinates from Global Energy Observatory record for Jinjie Coal Power Plant.

name: China Resources Haifeng CCS (Shanwei, Guangdong)
source: GEM Wiki: Shanwei Haifeng CCS power station; SCCS project info 1301 (Haifeng Power Plant CCS Plan); Guangdong Province CCUS demonstration listing 2023; ADB CCS Roadmap PRC 2022
notes: China Resources Power Haifeng coal-fired power plant CCS (4,100 MW+ station). Small pilot (20,000 tpa capture) operational since ~2019. Full-scale 1 Mtpa CCS expansion designated as Guangdong Province CCUS demonstration unit; reported in 'rapid construction' Sep 2023. CO2 to be injected into deep saline formation in South China Sea (~90-150 km offshore) or CNOOC EOR sites. Location: Xiaomo Town Da'ao Village, Haifeng, Shanwei.

name: Saudi Aramco Uthmaniyah CO2-EOR (Ghawar, Saudi Arabia)
source: MIT CCS project: Uthmaniyah; CSLF project profile; Aramco/KAPSARC announcement Jul 2015; OGCI CCUS Saudi Arabia report 2021; Aramco Europe CCUS page
notes: Saudi Arabia's first CCS project, operational Jul 2015. Captures 0.8 Mtpa CO2 from Hawiyah NGL Recovery Plant; compressed, dehydrated, transported ~85 km pipeline to Uthmaniyah sector of Ghawar oil field for CO2-EOR in Jurassic carbonate limestone. First large-scale CCS-EOR project in Middle East. Coordinates approximate for Uthmaniyah sector of Ghawar (~24.65°N, 49.15°E).

name: ADNOC Habshan CCUS (Al Gharbia, UAE)
source: ADNOC FID/investment announcement 2023; ADNOC Gas $615M contract award 2023; Shell Cansolv technology press release 2023; AGBI Nov 2024 (go-ahead); JPT 2023
notes: One of MENA's largest integrated CCS projects. FID by ADNOC Gas 2023. Captures 1.5 Mtpa CO2 at Habshan gas processing plant (Al Gharbia, Abu Dhabi), injects into Bab Far North geological formations ~150 miles SW. Uses Shell Cansolv absorption technology. Commissioning expected 2026. Distinct from existing Al Reyadah CCS (steel sector, 0.8 Mtpa). Coordinates: Habshan gas complex at ~23°49'N, 53°37'E (Wikipedia/Habshan article).

name: QatarEnergy Ras Laffan CCS
source: SCCS Edinburgh project info 2724; Carbon Herald 2024; QatarEnergy CCS press releases; Blackridge Research 2024; Anadolu Agency (5M tpa by 2025 target)
notes: Operational since ~2019 (earlier smaller-scale phases from 2012). Captures CO2 from LNG and gas processing at Ras Laffan Industrial City (~80 km N of Doha); injects into Arab Formation geological storage. Current active capacity 2.2 Mtpa; cumulative ~7.5 Mt stored to date. Expansion plans: 7-9 Mtpa by 2030, >11 Mtpa by 2035. World's largest CCS in LNG sector. New 4.1 Mtpa plant under construction. Coordinates: Ras Laffan Industrial City (25.93°N, 51.61°E).

name: Petronas Kasawari CCS (Block SK316, Sarawak, Malaysia)
source: NSEnergy Business Kasawari CCS project profile; Petronas FID Nov 2022; The Edge Malaysia 2025 (EPCIC contract RM4.5B to MMHE Aug 2025); Offshore Energy 2022; GEM Wiki Kasawari field (lat 4.981731, lon 112.972289)
notes: Positioned as world's largest offshore CCS project by volume. FID Nov 2022. Located in Block SK316, Central Luconia Province, ~200 km offshore Bintulu, Sarawak, ~108 m water depth. High-CO2 gas field; CO2 captured offshore and reinjected into M1 depleted field at 3.3 Mtpa. Start-up targeted end-2025/2026 (some sources cite 2027 push). First CCS project in Malaysia. Coordinates from GEM Wiki.

name: BP Tangguh CCUS / UCC (Bintuni Bay, West Papua, Indonesia)
source: BP FID press release Nov 2024; Offshore Energy Nov 2024 ($7B project); BP Indonesia Tangguh EGR/CCUS webpage; Global Energy Observatory (lat -2.44314, lon 133.139)
notes: FID November 2024 as part of $7B Tangguh Upstream Compression and CCS (UCC) project. Indonesia's most advanced CCUS project. Bintuni Bay, West Papua. Initial phase targets ~15 Mt total CO2 sequestration from Tangguh LNG operations; ultimate reservoir storage capacity ~1.8 Gt. CO2 injected back into gas reservoir for EGR + permanent storage. Production / injection start ~2028. Coordinates from Global Energy Observatory for Tangguh LNG plant.

name: Project Tundra (Minnkota Power, North Dakota, USA)
source: NDIC NDIC approvals announcement; DOE FONSI EA-2197 Sep 2024; Grand Forks Herald 2023 (up to $350M DOE grant); Project Tundra website projecttundrand.com; GetAMap (lat 47.0658, lon -101.2131)
notes: Would be world's largest post-combustion CO2 capture and geologic storage project. Captures up to 90% of CO2 from Minnkota Power's Milton R. Young lignite-fired station (Oliver County, ND) = ~4 Mtpa. Injects into Broom Creek (~1 mile deep) and Deadwood formations (~2 miles). Class VI permits approved by NDIC 2022. Up to $350M DOE grant (Dec 2023). FID pending federal/state economic framework. Coordinates from GetAMap for Milton R. Young Station.

name: Red Trail Energy CCS / Gevo North Dakota (Richardton, ND, USA)
source: Ethanol Producer Magazine Jun 2022; EcoEngineers project spotlight; Puro.earth 2024 CORC audit; Gevo/Red Trail acquisition press release Sep 2024; Decarb Connect project profile
notes: First US project to generate Puro.earth CO2 Removal Certificates (CORCs) for ethanol fermentation CCS (Mar 2024). Operational since Jun 2022. 64 MGY corn ethanol plant near Richardton, ND. Captures ~100% of fermentation CO2 (~180,000 tpa); injects via Class VI well into Broom Creek Formation ~6,500 ft below surface. Acquired by Gevo Inc. for $210M in Sep 2024. Coordinates approximate for Richardton, ND (46.88°N, 102.31°W).

name: Core Energy CO2-EOR (Northern Niagaran Reef Trend, Michigan, USA)
source: MIT CCS Otsego Northern Reef Trend fact sheet; NETL MRCSP Phase II Michigan Basin; EPA MRV reports Core Energy 2018-2021; MRCSP coordinates (45.042960, -84.482461)
notes: Operational since ~2003. CO2-EOR in Niagaran Pinnacle Reef Trend, Michigan Basin (~6,000 ft depth). CO2 sourced from DTE Energy Antrim Shale gas processing plant (~1,000 tpa/day = ~0.18 Mtpa). Cumulative injection hundreds of thousands of tonnes. One of the longest-running CO2-EOR with permanent storage monitoring in the US. Coordinates from NETL MRCSP Phase II (Otsego County, State-Charlton 30/31 field).

name: Stratos DAC + CCS (1PointFive / Occidental, Ector County, Texas, USA)
source: 1PointFive Ector County project page; Oxy/EPA Class VI permits approval Apr 2025; OGJ Apr 2025; RBN Energy 2025 (Q2 2026 startup target); Oxy news release
notes: World's largest Direct Air Capture facility. Located near Odessa/Penwell, Ector County, west Texas. Captures up to 0.5 Mtpa CO2 from atmosphere. Three Class VI injection wells (BRP CCS1-3) permitted Apr 2025; first-ever Class VI permits for DAC-sourced CO2. Inject into Permian Lower Sand Andres Formation, ~4,500-5,100 ft depth, near Penwell. Phase 1 startup target Q2 2026. Two capture trains in wet commissioning as of 2025. Coordinates approximate for Ector County/Odessa area.

name: Shell Polaris CCS / Atlas Carbon Storage Hub (Scotford, Alberta, Canada)
source: Shell FID press release Jun 2024; Alberta Major Projects (Polaris and Atlas entries); Canada.ca deputy PM visit Aug 2024; World Oil Jun 2024; Shell Scotford GEO coordinates (53.80137, -113.08992)
notes: FID Jun 2024. Shell Polaris captures ~650,000 tpa CO2 from Scotford refinery and chemicals complex (~40 km NE of Edmonton, AB). CO2 transported via 22 km pipeline to ATCO EnPower's Atlas Carbon Storage Hub, stored >2 km underground in Basal Cambrian Sands. Atlas Hub Phase 1: capacity 0.65 Mtpa; lifetime storage potential ~300 Mt. Operations expected late 2028. Creates ~2,000 jobs. Coordinates from Global Energy Observatory for Shell Scotford complex.

name: Pathways Alliance (Oil Sands Alliance) CCS Hub (Cold Lake, Alberta, Canada)
source: Oil Sands Alliance project overview (rebrand Feb 2026); Global CCS Institute Pathways profile Oct 2025; Alberta Energy Regulator application Mar 2024; Pathways Alliance fact sheet Jun 2025
notes: Canada's largest proposed CCS project ($16.5B). 400 km CO2 pipeline from >20 oil sands facilities in NE Alberta (Fort McMurray/Cold Lake region) to underground storage hub near Cold Lake. CO2 injected into Basal Cambrian Sandstone 1,000-2,000 m depth. Target: 22 Mtpa CO2 reduction by 2030. AER regulatory application filed Mar 2024. FID contingent on federal/provincial economic framework. Partners: Canadian Natural Resources, Cenovus, ConocoPhillips, Imperial Oil, MEG Energy, Suncor (>95% oil sands production). Coordinates approximate for Cold Lake storage hub.

name: Enbridge Open Access Wabamun Carbon Hub (Alberta, Canada)
source: Enbridge press releases 2022-2025; Alberta Major Projects: Open Access Wabamun Carbon Hub; Enbridge milestones page Oct 2025; Alberta Innovates support spring 2024
notes: Enbridge awarded evaluation rights 2022 (~220,000 ha west/north of Edmonton). Two appraisal wells drilled 2023-2024. Sequestration agreement signed Oct 2025 for North Area Basal Sandstone Unit. Open-access utility-scale CO2 storage. Currently contracted: Heidelberg Materials Edmonton Capture Project (~1.3 Mtpa). Also serves Air Products Canada Net-zero Hydrogen Energy Complex. FID not yet reached. Coordinates approximate for Wabamun Lake area (~53.5°N, 114.5°W).

name: ExxonMobil Baytown Low-Carbon Hydrogen + CCS (Texas, USA)
source: ExxonMobil Baytown hydrogen viewpoint (corporate.exxonmobil.com); CCUS Expo 2024; Calpine/ExxonMobil CO2 T&S agreement Apr 2025; ExxonMobil CCS business (16 Mtpa under contract, 2025)
notes: Proposed $7B low-carbon hydrogen plant at Baytown complex, TX. Would produce 1 Bcf/d low-carbon hydrogen and capture 7.5 Mtpa CO2 for permanent storage. FID expected 2024 but facing delays/potential cancellation following May 2025 DOE CCS grant cancellations ($3.7B revoked). Part of ExxonMobil TX/LA/MS CCS pipeline network (Denbury acquisition 2023; ~16 Mtpa under CCS contract as of Apr 2025). Status: planned/at-risk. Coordinates approximate for Baytown refining complex (~29.74°N, 95.0°W).

name: CO2CRC Otway International Test Centre (Victoria, Australia)
source: Global Energy Observatory record 40159 (lat -38.553394, lon 142.89484); CO2CRC Otway Project website; CSIRO Otway page; CSLF Stage 2 project profile
notes: Australia's key CCS research and demonstration site. Designated Australian National Research Asset. Near Port Campbell, SW Victoria (~300 km from Melbourne). CO2 injected into depleted Waarre C natural gas reservoir ~2 km depth since 2008. Over 65,000 t stored in initial phase; subsequent monitoring and injection phases ongoing. Not commercial scale; capacity estimate reflects ongoing research injection rate (~50 ktpa). Coordinates from Global Energy Observatory.

name: Bayu-Undan CCS (Santos, Timor Sea, Timor-Leste)
source: Santos press releases 2022-2024; Offshore Magazine 2022 (FEED initiated); IEEFA Bayu-Undan CCS analysis 2024; ICN Gateway project 4891; Bayu-Undan field: ~500 km from Darwin in Timor Sea
notes: Santos plans to repurpose depleted Bayu-Undan gas/condensate field (Timor Sea, ~500 km from Darwin, ~250 km S of Timor-Leste) as regional CO2 storage hub. Potential 10 Mtpa capacity (~1 Mtpa initial); MoUs signed for CO2 supply. FEED nearly complete. Target ready 2028 (Santos purchasing interim carbon credits). Positioned as regional storage hub for Asian industrial emitters. IEEFA (2024) flagged commercial/financing challenges. Timor-Leste JV partner: TIMOR GAP. Coordinates approximate for Bayu-Undan field.

name: Northern Lights CCS Phase 2 Expansion (Norway)
source: TotalEnergies Phase 2 announcement Mar 2025; Northern Lights website; Equinor Smeaheia page
notes: Capacity increment above Phase 1 (already in existing dataset as 'Northern Lights CCS Phase 1' at 1.5 Mtpa). Phase 2 announced Mar 2025 increases total injection from 1.5 to minimum 5.0 Mtpa (+3.5 Mtpa net new). First CO2 received Aug 2025. Storage same location ~2,500 m below North Sea seabed south of Troll field. Owned equally by TotalEnergies, Equinor, Shell. Coordinate same as Phase 1 entry.

name: Equinor Smeaheia CO2 Storage (North Sea, Norway)
source: Equinor Smeaheia project page; OGJ Apr 2022 (license awarded); Offshore Magazine 2024 (two appraisal wells drilled late 2024); Equinor award press release Apr 2022
notes: License awarded to Equinor (100% WI) Apr 2022. Large saline aquifer east of Troll field, offshore west Norway. Two appraisal wells completed late 2024. Ambition: up to 20 Mtpa CO2 from 2030s; potential to serve major European industrial clusters. Target reservoirs in Jurassic Viking and Dunlin Groups. CO2 pipeline to connect to Smeaheia and adjacent stores (Kinno, Abondigas). First injection expected early 2030s. Distinct from existing Sleipner/Snøhvit/Northern Lights sites.

name: Offshore Western Kyushu CCS (ENEOS / JX Nippon / J-POWER, Japan)
source: JOGMEC Advanced CCS Projects selection FY2024; ENEOS/JX Nippon/J-POWER/West Japan Carbon press release Oct 2024; Enerdata 2024; TankTerminals.com 2024; METI CCS commercialisation report Jun 2024
notes: One of nine JOGMEC-selected Advanced CCS Projects (Japan, FY2024). Consortium: ENEOS, JX Nippon Oil & Gas Exploration, J-POWER, West Japan Carbon. Engineering design and exploratory drilling phase post-FY2023 feasibility. 1.7 Mtpa CO2 from refineries and thermal power plants in Setouchi and Kyushu regions, transported by ship/pipeline to offshore saline aquifers west of Kyushu. FID target FY2026; operations by 2030. Coordinates approximate for planned offshore saline storage area W of Kyushu.
